speaker: interviewer
text: Some people never had access to them. You-- you are very eloquent, as I said, and articulate. We are-- as interviewers, we're not supposed to jump in with a word when we see the subject--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because, first of all, your word-- especially in your case-- is going to be better. When you-- you know, when-- when you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I know. I know that you are--

speaker: interviewer
text: Continuing with Dr. Herbert Zipper talking to Sandy Jacobson in Los Angeles, California in English, 9th February 1995. And this is tape number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Zipper, please talk about-- you referred to the magnificence of human beings. How-- how can you have magnificence in a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh! Sure. Those who are the magnificent ones-- they light up immediately. They are the people who have the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] immediate and strong urge to help others, to save them from the worst. And the worst, in the magnificent people's minds, is to commit suicide. That means to do just exactly what they want us to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To help others not to give up-- to help others to have a sense of humor-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is what I call the leader of men who really have the gift, even if sometimes if they have never done it before. I mention in the book one person, Mr. Tillinger. I remember his name so well. But, of course, there were others. Little Tillinger who was a 50-year-old seller of second-rate vegetables in a little subterranean shop in Vienna in a-- in a second-rate district. Who eked out his life by selling these things and wrapping them in newsprint. Who didn't go beyond fourth-- third grade or fourth grade. Who had a hard time writing or reading. This Tillinger became suddenly a great man who helped. I'm sure there are scores of people who owe their life to Tillinger, who helped them to grow up and to be themselves and to have-- and not to give in and to describe what these-- these gangsters are, I mean, with speech. And he taught me. I didn't know I can do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Talk about how you conducted concerts, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, Tillinger became one of my teachers there. I mean, teachers, not-- not that we had lessons, you see. But teachers-- that I saw what he was doing and how he was doing it and that this-- this is the way-- this is the way to-- to live there-- to not think about ever getting out alive. You see, this was the-- the thing that I learned very soon. I had no idea that I will get out of this mess ever alive. So I have to live here and now I'm going to make that life as livable as possible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I learned in the-- in the-- from the very beginning by reciting poetry, which I had quite a body of poetry by-- by heart. That's a question of-- that was my question of education. It's nothing special. And then I saw that this poetry begun to mean a great deal to many people who have never listened to poetry or who have never read poetry.

speaker: interviewer
text: You stood outside of your barracks and recited poetry?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. This was in the first-- first or second night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people came-- gathered around you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gathered around, and-- and-- and their whole spirit started suddenly different-- you see, what it is really-- it is not conscious. But what it really is is that suddenly they heard some human words, some human expression of excellence. And so I began to understand it. And this excellence really made them feel also like human beings again. Their self-respect began to increase. That's-- I mean, that's-- I presume that this was this. That's one factor, at least. There is this language that they suddenly understood that was not their everyday language. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the same thing is with music is-- is we forget that the greatest musical expressions are communications with one's self. This cannot be replaced by anything else. You cannot replace a great musical line-- what it does to you-- by anything else. Therefore, it is a communication that is irreplaceable. And people forget that, and people who do not-- are not being taught early in life that this great communication is one side of life, which must also take part in the daily life or in-- in the natural, everyday life. That this is not something special which you do on certain occasions, but it is part of-- of the-- the need for something, which nothing else can fill but the great-- the great expressions of music.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And so if you do-- to be able to do something which expresses their-- their inner-- inner emotional-- like, it's difficult to say these words, because this-- otherwise, we wouldn't need music. That this need is an innate one in the human race, regardless where it comes from. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that told me to-- to write music and to make those music instruments with my-- there were some extraordinary people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you make musical instruments in a concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, first of all, we had two or three first-rate music instrument makers in-- as-- as one of the prisoners from Munich. They couldn't do-- of course, they didn't have the right tools. But they could make some things that sounded like music. Mostly string instruments, you know. The cases where you put the string on top of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you get the string?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was-- didn't you read in the book?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but the people watching the tape didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I see. Well, I saw one of those SS men very soon who was shouting like mad, but was never doing anything mad-- anything bad. And one day, I thought, this is-- must be a Communist spy or something like this. Because the behavior-- also his knowledge of words-- [? his ?] words were different from those. I said, this is a socialist. This is not a Communist.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I approach him on day. I said, [INAUDIBLE]-- I said, listen. We are making some music instruments. I said so under my breath. And we need some strings. And I described the strings. And he didn't say a word. He didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: But a few weeks later, I found a whole bunch of usable strings under my pillow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is where I got strings. That's the way we made music.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you make the music?

speaker: interviewee
text: There in a [INAUDIBLE] latrine. It is 10 toilets. I could see if-- I could see a movie-- some people sitting on the empty toilets in the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how could you get-- I mean, you couldn't get everybody in at one time.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we could get-- the most that we could get in about 25 to 30 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We played a short poem of 15 minutes all afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: You would play one performance, and then these people would leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they went down. They come in. And there were also people watching, whether there's an SS, but all coming.

words.text: they went down. They come in.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: On Sundays. That was the only time you could do it-- Sunday afternoon. It was your-- your so-called free time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have musicians there, or did you teach people how to play certain instruments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Listen, my dear. We had at least 30 or 40 excellent musicians who were in jail there. They-- they were mostly string players, but they used this-- I mean, really talented people-- professionals. And, of course, I worked with professionals there. I didn't have to teach them anything. They-- they could sight-read. They could immediately-- we had a rehearsal immediately after 1 o'clock-- rehearsal for about half an hour, just go through with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I wrote on this paper that they gave me-- it was very clear always There was-- there was always a discussion of do you mean G-sharp or G-flat? So you would write out the arrangements for each instrument?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wouldn't write a score. I would write immediately the parts. I didn't have to add the score. So I learned-- also what I learned there is to rely on my memory. And not to use-- have to use an instrument to know what it sound-- sounds like. You can press yourself to all kinds of things that you didn't learn-- that was not in your-- in your curriculum. But when the need arises, your potentials come to your help.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about 'Dachaulied,' the 'Dachau Song.' Tell me how that got written.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't-- didn't write it down at all. That was too dangerous to write words and music together. So I didn't write it all. Both Jura and I did not-- we did not write anything down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the words to it. Who wrote the words to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jura--

speaker: interviewer
text: Doctor, you have to watch your hand. If you brush the microphone, it goes [? crumble. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: Jura Soyfer, who was one of the writers with whom I did work already in Vienna. And he wrote-- wrote-- the story is very simple. We-- when we were both at that time horses on a-- on the cement-- cement truck. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: By horses you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went through that door of Dachau day-- every day many times. And there was this huge inscription-- [SPEAKING GERMAN], Work makes free. And one day I got so mad. It was in hot August. I said, Jura, this is really too much that-- to-- to write this thing on top of there. By the way, I got yesterday from-- from the Holocaust Museum in Washington-- one of their-- their weekly-- and there's one-- one picture of Auschwitz where there is also a very-- very large the same-- the same, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said here-- he said, you know, we should write a song. I said, OK. He said, I already think of it. And about a few days later, he came in to recite it to me. And I memorized it in-- on one day of-- on one trip. I have quite a good memory. I can memorize things very-- especially if somebody tells it to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think it was two or three days later, I-- I sang it to him. And he says, oh, that sounds good to me. And then I taught it to one of the musicians. And they accompanied to-- to guitars. And then we left to Buchenwald. And that's all I knew about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: But many-- there's a peculiar thing of-- of some things. I just wrote it for the people-- some kind of resistance, sometimes to give them hope and-- but if things begin to arouse the imagination of people-- whatever it is-- they begin to have their own life. I think 'Dachau'-- the 'Dachau Song' had-- had its own life without-- without me. Without its-- and lived by the word of mouth-- from-- from east to west, from south to north, in every concentration camp and later also in Russia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- the numbers of-- of-- of arrangements I got were very peculiar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's talk a little bit about Buchenwald. You had-- you had a terrible job there in Buchenwald. You worked, as you called it, in the Eau de Cologne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, see, the-- yeah, cleaning-- cleaning of the latrines. You see, in-- in Buchenwald, the really horrifying thing was that there was no water. Absolutely no water, period. We didn't have any water. We couldn't wash. We couldn't have-- cleaning our teeth. There was no-- we couldn't clean anything. There was no water. So therefore, there were no toilets. There were only open latrines. That means deep-- deep ditches that you have-- there were some wood planks on-- on side of it. That's where people did their business. And, of course, when you think that there were 10,000 people in all the [INAUDIBLE], there would only two or three latrines there. So they were pretty full every day, and we had-- we had the business of emptying them. We had these five gallon-- I think it must have been five gallon. Yeah, it was about 10 liters or something like that. Cans, which you had on strings. Let it down, filled it up, and walked with it. And, of course, on the sides, the things began-- so after a couple of days, we start to smell from far away. And my brother coined the word, the Eau de Cologne.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you had a night there where you had to stand all night. Will you talk about that? The night in December where--

speaker: interviewee
text: December. It was December 1938. It was very cold. We were sub-- subzero temperature. It's 5 o'clock. The-- the appell place had to be filled. We came in there, and the-- the head men there all told us that two prisoners escaped and that we have to stand there until they are captured. See, this is one thing which people do not understand, and even Hannah Arendt doesn't-- didn't understand. That there was something worse than death, and it's torture. Whatever any one did-- every one of the 10,000 people had to suffer for it. It didn't-- that was not new. But I can only emphasize that this was what-- what many of us did not even contemplate to do anything against the rules of the-- because we knew very well that it will be will be a dreadful thing for everyone else. So there we stood from 5 o'clock to next day [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 12 o'clock noon. For 19 hours. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Many people died of frostbite, of freezing-- many old people, especially. Old people-- who might to say somebody who's 60 is old? They died, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] most of the people had very heavy frostbites. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a-- but a very peculiar night. If you-- I would recommend that you read Bruno Bettelheim's description of that night, because he does a phenomenal job on the-- towards the last hours, how they simply start integrate-- disintegrated. How-- how there was a kind of hilarity that we created-- that even the SS-- something had completely changed during that time-- how people just said, OK, I'm going to die. I'm dying. So-- so what? It was-- it was a peculiar-- but as I said, when I read it-- actually, Bruno told me again about it. That was the best description of what-- of the spirit that it created. Something entirely different than what it-- could be expected. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was really terrible, but again, I learned something, which I would have never learned otherwise. What? How one can control oneself-- and do things oneself that one didn't know one could do-- control one's blood-- blood circulation. How'd you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just by not stopping to move for one second any part of your body, so that you can see-- not see it from the outside, but that you still do it. And that's what I did for the 19 hours. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Everybody can do it, if you have the goal. You see, I was afraid about my hands, because I-- for a great part, I lived by my hands-- if they are really-- become useless, as many of them became-- so I-- I thought anyway I'm going to-- I'm going to try to not to stop the-- the movement that gave me-- gave me the possibility of having the blood circulation. And I didn't get any frostbite, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother did?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother did, very heavy. For three or four months, he couldn't wear one shoe.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was a man, a Jew, who was an informant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Z. And they-- the people in your barracks wanted to kill him, and you wouldn't let them. Talk about that a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that's not right. That's wrong. Nobody wanted to kill him. The-- the SS wanted to kill him, because he-- he-- he informed on the SS. That was his stupidity. He was a Viennese lawyer who didn't want to get through that type of work of Dachau and Buchenwald and in the very, very beginning decided to become an informer. And we knew it very soon. And we had to stay away from him, because he-- it was a great danger to talk to him. No, he-- he went so stupidly to the-- to the SS heads and told them about the business deeds that the SS was doing. And they, of course, went immediately back and-- and tried to kill him. And that's what they did finally.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had-- you-- you wrote about a time where the people in your barracks were-- were attacking him. And you said, don't do this, because you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't say that. That was something else. No, he was-- the things-- Jura and I who wanted to get him-- he was heavily, heavily beaten and wounded. And I mean, he was really-- and he became suddenly a human being, asking for his mother. And neither Jura nor I could stand that. So we took him on the-- on the stretcher and brought him to the-- the prisoners' hospital. But the prisoners wouldn't-- wouldn't take him. That's it. That's the story. They refused. So we put him back to our barrack. There we had to leave him outside on the-- on-- not-- not outside in the outside, but outside of-- of our rooms. Our-- and that's where he was killed by the SS-- with sand sacks. It's a nice way to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: Something that you did back in Dachau I thought was very funny. One day you decided that you'd worked quite enough-- you'd been a horse, as you've put it, quite enough. So you got yourself a measuring stick.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you mean the yard stick. Oh!

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell that story.

speaker: interviewee
text: The yard stick? Well, I found that yard stick. It was just some-- somebody lost it on the-- and I thought, this is a good-- a good tool, a tool for freedom, because I'm-- the Prussian spirit is a spirit that has to be lived. It cannot be explained. Anything that is-- looks like official-- anything that looks like something that has been ordered to do-- that has nothing to do with work of prisoner is correct. That's that-- that Prussian spirit. You cannot explain it exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- but I've learned it. So I took that next morning from the work-- went away and started measuring every distance whenever I saw an officer coming up. And they passed by, didn't pay any attention to me, because I was measuring something with an official tool. So now we'll-- we'll go ahead-- as much as I would love to hear the details, I know you're tiring. Now talk about-- your father was in England, and he was able to arrange for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he was in England, but he went to Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was he able to-- he was able to arrange?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, the thing was that the rules were-- it took quite some time until-- until the-- the rules of-- of-- of exodus of Jews was allowed. It was, I think, by December or January that these rules were promulgated, announced.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of the men who was in-- who was hanged in Israel? The-- name-- I forget the name. Who did--

speaker: interviewee
text: The German-- the German who--

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't mean Schindler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what name did you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Schindler.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not Schindler. The man who was hanged.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Eichmann.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eichmann, yeah. Eichmann was in charge in Vienna about the whole thing. And he got the rules from Germany, and it was then known that one could get out of a concentration camp if-- if I was able to immigrate into a foreign country-- foreign country outside of Europe and outside of the United States. That means it could have been South America. It could have been Asia and so forth. And my father-- I think he bribed some officer of the Guatemala Embassy in Paris to have two visas issued for my brother and for me. And this was sent to the Gestapo in Vienna. You know what the Gestapo was. And then they issued the release for-- for us to leave. That was the way we could get out. Of course, the moment the war started, it was-- it was over.

speaker: interviewer
text: That changed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was--
